skill.name: shadcn-ui
skill.description: Guides developers through shadcn/ui setup, component discovery, installation, customization, migration, and accessibility-aware UI composition.
skill.summary: Build and customize React interfaces with shadcn/ui components and project-specific guidance.
skill.author: google-labs-code

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the weak cryptography, Ruby backtick, and path traversal alerts are false positives from Markdown, Tailwind class names, and documentation links. Real residual risk is medium because the skill recommends npm and npx package installation, allows Bash and web_fetch, and includes a local shell verification script.

medium_findings.title: External Package Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users to run npx and npm commands that download packages or components from external registries. This is legitimate for shadcn/ui setup, but users should verify package names and run commands in trusted project directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit package-manager commands and are central to the skill workflow. They appear legitimate, but they execute third-party tooling from package registries.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions for Setup Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill metadata allows Read, Write, Bash, and web_fetch. These permissions fit a UI setup assistant, but they increase impact if a future prompt or registry response is untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools are directly listed in the skill metadata. No malicious use was found, but the permission set supports file changes, shell commands, and network retrieval.

low_findings.title: Local Shell Verification Script
low_findings.description: The included verification script uses find, grep, and command substitution to inspect local project configuration. It does not execute user-controlled shell strings, but it reads the working tree and should be run only in an intended project directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script commands are visible and quote discovered file paths before use. The behavior is read-only setup verification, so the residual concern is limited.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto alerts correspond to words and Tailwind or component identifiers such as CardDescription, md, and destructive. No hashing, encryption, or weak cryptographic API use was found in the reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed flagged lines contain UI component names, comments, CSS color labels, or Tailwind breakpoint text. There is no crypto library call or algorithm selection at those locations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution findings are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced command examples. The Markdown text itself does not execute commands, though some documented commands are separately covered as package-installation risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is Markdown documentation, not Ruby code. Execution would require a user or agent to intentionally run the shown commands.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are Markdown links to repository files and examples. They do not perform filesystem reads or writes by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation links containing ../ segments. No code opens these paths or combines them with user input.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to expected shadcn/ui, GitHub, Radix UI, Tailwind CSS, and related documentation resources. No evidence found of credential exfiltration or suspicious endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references and match the skill domain. The only residual concern is normal external navigation risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Execution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple files provide npm and npx commands for creating projects, installing dependencies, adding shadcn/ui components, and running migrations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are explicit external commands. They are expected for this skill, but command execution from documentation should remain user-approved.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Uses Local Discovery Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The verification script uses find and grep to locate CSS, component, and utility files in the current project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real shell execution, but no destructive command or unquoted command injection vector was found in these lines.

content.value_statement: Teams spend time finding the right shadcn/ui components and wiring setup details correctly. This skill gives structured guidance for setup, component selection, customization, migration, and quality checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides shadcn/ui initialization for new and existing React projects.
Explains how to add components and blocks with the shadcn CLI or MCP tools.
Provides examples for forms, authentication layouts, and data tables.
Documents theme customization with CSS variables, Tailwind configuration, and variants.
Maps migration paths from Material UI, Chakra UI, Ant Design, and Bootstrap.
Includes a shell script that checks common setup files and dependencies.
limitations: It does not include the shadcn/ui component source itself.
Package installation commands still depend on external registries and user approval.
Examples are templates and must be adapted to each project architecture.
Accessibility guidance does not replace manual testing with real application content.

use_cases.title: Initialize a React UI Stack
use_cases.description: Set up shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, aliases, and base components in a new project.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers starting a React application

use_cases.title: Standardize Product Components
use_cases.description: Choose component patterns for forms, tables, dialogs, navigation, dashboards, and authentication flows.
use_cases.target_user: Product engineers building reusable interface patterns

use_cases.title: Plan a UI Library Migration
use_cases.description: Compare existing Material UI, Chakra UI, Ant Design, or Bootstrap components with shadcn/ui equivalents.
use_cases.target_user: Technical leads migrating an existing frontend

prompt_templates.title: Set Up shadcn/ui
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup in an existing project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up shadcn/ui in my React project. Check my framework, Tailwind setup, components.json, and import aliases before suggesting commands.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Component Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a shadcn/ui form pattern for this feature. Include validation, accessible labels, error messages, and the components I need to install.

prompt_templates.title: Customize the Theme
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me customize shadcn/ui for my brand. Define CSS variables, Tailwind extensions, component variants, and dark mode considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate a UI Surface
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a migration from my current UI library to shadcn/ui for this page. Map components, identify risks, and propose an incremental rollout.

output_examples.input: Help me add a data table with sorting and filtering.
output: Recommended components: table, button, input, and dropdown menu.
Recommended dependency: TanStack Table for row models, sorting, filtering, and pagination.
Implementation outline: define typed rows, create column definitions, wire table state, then add controls for filters and column visibility.

output_examples.input: Customize my shadcn/ui buttons for a success action.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest adding a success variant in the button variant configuration, checking contrast, documenting usage, and testing hover, disabled, and dark mode states.

output_examples.input: Move a login form from Material UI to shadcn/ui.
output_examples.output: The skill would map TextField to Input plus Label, replace layout helpers with Tailwind utilities, keep semantic form structure, and preserve validation behavior.

best_practices: Review npm and npx commands before running them, especially in production repositories.
Keep copied shadcn/ui primitives small and compose project-specific wrappers outside the ui folder.
Test keyboard navigation, focus states, color contrast, and responsive behavior after customization.
anti_patterns: Do not blindly run package-manager commands from generated guidance without checking package names.
Do not remove ARIA attributes, labels, or focus handling when changing component markup.
Do not force a full UI migration when an incremental page-by-page rollout is safer.

faq.question: Does this skill install shadcn/ui automatically?
faq.answer: It provides commands and guidance. Users should approve and run installation commands in the correct project directory.

faq.question: Can it work with both Radix UI and Base UI?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance describes choosing the primitive library during setup and adapting component usage to that choice.

faq.question: Is this only for Next.js projects?
faq.answer: No. It includes guidance for Next.js, Vite, and general React project setup.

faq.question: Can it help with design system customization?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers CSS variables, Tailwind extensions, variants, typography, spacing, and dark mode.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The reviewed content is not malicious, but it recommends external package commands and includes a local shell verification script.

faq.question: Does it replace accessibility testing?
faq.answer: No. It explains accessible patterns, but teams still need manual and automated testing in their real application.
